g.data-narration: Biconnected components help identify critical points in graphs that could disconnect the network if removed.
g.text: Articulation Points, Bridges andamp DFS Algorithm

g.data-narration: Networks must remain connected even when nodes fail. An articulation point is a single point of failure.
g.text: Why Do We Need Biconnected Components?
Networks must remain connected even when nodes fail. An articulation point is a
single point of failure - if it fails, the network breaks into disconnected parts.

g.data-narration: Three key concepts: articulation points (cut vertices), bridges (cut edges), and biconnected components.
A vertex v is an articulation point if removing it disconnects the graph into multiple components.
g.text: A vertex v is an articulation point if removing v (and its incident edges)
increases the number of connected components in the graph.

g.data-narration: A bridge is an edge whose removal disconnects the graph. Every bridge belongs to exactly one component.
g.text: An edge e = (u, v) is a bridge if removing e increases the number of
connected components. A bridge is always part of exactly one biconnected component.

g.data-narration: A biconnected graph has no articulation points - every pair of vertices has two disjoint paths.
g.text: A connected graph with NO articulation points. Equivalently: for every pair
of vertices, there exist at least two vertex-disjoint paths between them.

g.data-narration: Biconnected components are maximal subgraphs with no cut vertices. Edges belong to exactly one component.
g.text: A maximal biconnected subgraph. Key properties:
- Every edge belongs to exactly ONE component
- Two components share at most ONE vertex (an articulation point)

g.data-narration: For this graph, vertices 1 and 3 are articulation points, creating three biconnected components.
DFS uses two arrays: disc records when vertices are first visited, low tracks reachable discovery times.
g.text: Two arrays track vertex discovery during DFS:
disc[v] = Discovery time of v (when first visited)
low[v] = Lowest discovery time reachable from v's subtree

g.data-narration: Low value is minimum of the vertex discovery time, back edge discovery times, and child low values.
g.text: For each vertex v, low[v] is the minimum of three values:
low[v] = min(disc[v], disc[w] for back edges, low[child] for tree edges)

g.data-narration: Root is articulation if it has multiple children. Non-root is articulation if child low is at least parent disc.
g.text: A vertex u is an articulation point if ANY of these conditions hold:
Condition 1 (Root): u is DFS root AND has 2 or more children
Condition 2 (Non-root): exists child v with low[v] >= disc[u]
if (parent[u] != -1 andamp andamp low[v] gt= disc[u])

g.data-narration: Bridges use strict greater than: low[child] must be strictly greater than parent discovery time.
g.text: An edge (u, v) where v is a child of u in DFS tree is a BRIDGE if:

g.data-narration: Use an edge stack during DFS. When articulation point found, pop edges to form one component.
g.text: Use a stack of edges during DFS. When articulation point detected,
pop edges from stack to form one biconnected component.

g.data-narration: Block-cut tree is a bipartite graph showing how biconnected components connect through articulation points.
g.text: Bipartite graph: components (B) connected via articulation points (1,3)

g.data-narration: Key properties: edges belong to one component, components share at most one vertex, bridges are single-edge components.
All three computations (articulation points, bridges, components) run in linear time O(V plus E).
Applications include network reliability analysis, circuit design, transportation networks, and social network analysis.
Remember: disc and low computation, articulation conditions, and bridge uses strict greater than comparison.
g.text: Root: 2+ children | Non-root: low[child] gt= disc
Trace DFS by hand for small graphs!

g.data-narration: Articulation points are vertices whose removal disconnects graphs; bridges are edges with the same property.
g.text: - Bridge: edge whose removal disconnects the graph
